I'll implement the breakthrough idea: dose-dependent effective plateau. This targets the worst-curve/high-quantile shape term by capturing that strong inhibitors reduce total fibril mass, not just delay it. Let me make a targeted, disciplined change.

The physical principle: inhibitors that sequester monomer or block fibril surfaces don't only delay aggregation — at sufficient dose they lower the endpoint aggregate load (fewer competent fibril ends, depleted soluble monomer pool). This shifts the 90% response crossing and the late-time shape, which is exactly where the blended worst-curve shape term concentrates for delayed inhibitor curves.

I'll set `plateau = c0/(1 + c1^2*inhibitor)`, keep the monomer-sequestration source/secondary structure, and retain a bounded linear time-drag. This is a moderate 8-constant model — simpler than the current 10-constant version (which also helps the "simplify" focus area) while adding the dose-dependent endpoint the breakthrough calls for.
